renewals,

My name is Lucy Wateriss, Chief Executive of Clubs New Zealand.
We represent 300 clubs across the country,
ranging from small groups of a few dozen members
who might get together
a few times a week, through to large community hubs of
over 13,000 members.
Regardless of size, they are places where New Zealanders gather to
socialize, play sport, share meals, access
affordable entertainment, and find community.
Clubs New Zealand supports this bill.
We think it strikes the right balance between harm minimization and
practical improvements for responsible operators.
Clubs are generally low risk. They are member-governed, not
for profit, and deeply embedded within their communities.
That model naturally supports responsible service, and it is a
responsibility that we take seriously.
As always, I take our written submission as read, and in the
interest of time, want to focus today on why change is
needed, particularly for club licenses and special licenses.
The provision allowing clubs to choose between a club
license and a non-license introduces something that clubs have not
had in a very long time, and that is choice.
We strongly support this provision.
It provides a lawful and practical pathway for some
clubs to better meet community needs.
But it's important that it remains a genuine choice, and
not something that clubs feel pushed toward by reporting
agencies or because the existing settings are too
restrictive.
We do believe that clear transitional provisions
need to be included within the bill.
If a club chooses to move to a non-license,
it should be treated as a transition, not a new license, and
not a full reset. We do believe that its
compliance history should be retained.
While we strongly support
clubs having the opportunity to choose between a club license and a
non-license, we do believe that the underlying
issues are found within Section 60 of the act
itself, and they should be addressed as well.
In our view, Section 60 is the root cause of the many
challenges that clubs face today.
It restricts who a club can serve in ways that no
longer reflect how communities operate.
That, in turn, limits a club's ability to host events, attract
participation, and remain sustainable.
Clubs today are not exclusive and closed
environments. They are often the only venues for
community events, such as funerals, prize givings,
fundraisers, and local gatherings.
300,000 New Zealanders choose to belong to one or more of our
clubs, 4,000 people are employed within our clubs,
and more than 6,000 volunteers give their time
to our clubs every year.
Yet to meet community expectations, clubs are often forced
into what I would describe as complex workarounds of the law.
It is the restrictive nature of Section 60 that drives heavy
reliance on special licenses.
And I do want to be clear that clubs are not applying for special licenses
because they want to. They are doing so to meet community need and
to comply with the law. This often means applying for special
licenses for routine, low-risk events where alcohol is
incidental.
In some parts of the country, we've seen informal limits being applied
to how many special licenses a club can apply for in one
year. And you can just imagine the club
newsletter going out, asking people to please delay their funerals or
special occasions to the following year because the club
has hit its special license limit.
That sounds absurd to us, because it
is.While it may sound
macabre, funerals are actually a good example of where clubs
use special licenses. That they're regularly
hosting funerals for members of the club or the wider community,
and in many cases, the club is such an important part
of the member's life, it's where they choose to say farewell.
But funerals are not events that we book weeks in advance,
and the 20 working-day requirement for a special license is often
impractical. And while waivers exist, they come
with additional costs, and that adds stress
and cost at an already difficult time for people.
Another great example that I heard came from the House during the first reading
of the bill. There was discussion
around boating clubs needing special licenses for events like the Wotton Cup,
where one club hosts another and another club visits.
The issue here is not the participants in that boating event,
it's the
supporters, it's the family members, the friends, the wider community that turn
up to support the event. Those people are often not members
themselves of either clubs. So to remain
compliant, the club is left with little choice but to apply for a
special license to cover what is, in effect, a normal
community activity.
That is where the system becomes impractical because we're
requiring already licensed low-risk clubs to
repeatedly apply for special licenses for
events that we believe pose no additional risk.
We believe that there is a more practical approach through expanding the
definition of authorized visitor under Section 60 to
include any person attending a function or club event
similar to jurisdictions like Queensland.
Clubs are unique. We are not-for-profit, member-based
community venues. But we are not immune to the pressures
facing the wider hospitality sector.
In my role, I'm often asked why are clubs struggling, why are RSAs
folding.
And the reality is, there is no one single answer to that, but
perception plays a very big role. Imagine turning up
to a club for the first time, being faced with a closed door, a
doorbell, a swipe card access, a large members only
sign. I myself have been chased across a club by the
office lady. She was very well-intentioned, but asking me to sign the sign-in
book.
That is not welcoming, and it's a barrier before you've even stepped in the
door. But those barriers exist because clubs are trying to comply with
Section 60. At the same time, we
are trying to evolve. We have clubs trying to introduce new membership
categories. They are trying to be more open and accessible because
they know they need to attract new members to survive.
But too often those efforts are treated by reporting agencies with
suspicion, or they're interpreted as attempts to work around the law.
This creates a tension, and we need to rebalance the settings
so that clubs can genuinely welcome people in so that
becoming a member feels like a natural next step and not a
compliance exercise. Because ultimately, if clubs
cannot attract new members, they cannot remain sustainable.
And if they're not sustainable, then we lose that really important
community infrastructure that they provide.
So in summary,
we support this bill. We welcome the introduction of choice for clubs.
But we strongly encourage the committee to ensure that there are fair
transitional provisions for clubs that do choose a non-license.
Amend Section 60 to better reflect how clubs operate today
and address the practical realities of special licenses.
These changes would reduce unnecessary burden, improve
consistency, and ensure clubs can continue serving their communities in
a safe and responsible way. Thank you.

Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Lizzie Barrett toku
ingoa. I'm a health promotion advisor making this submission on behalf of
Alcohol Health Watch. We're an independent NGO and peak body
in Aotearoa with a focus on preventing and reducing alcohol-related
harm by strengthening public policy.
I'm joined by our executive director, Andrew Galloway.
Firstly, we give credit to our colleague, Sarah Sneed, who outlined the
extensive evidence against most of this bill's proposed changes in our
written submission. As you know, we oppose these changes because
they prioritize reducing red tape for the alcohol industry over
people's rights to health and wellbeing.
Today, we'd like to emphasize our collective responsibility for a
reasonable and evidence-based approach to alcohol law reform.
This should involve balancing the harms the alcohol industry causes against their
ability to profit, keeping in mind that they do so through
strategies that both normalize alcohol throughout our society and
strategically profit off heavy drinking.
As you've already heard this morning, the industry amplifies the harms of social
disadvantage, including poverty, gendered violence, trauma,
social isolation, and stigma and discrimination, just to name a few.
These dynamics mean that alcohol harm is a health, social, and justice
issue with many different causes.
But we can do something about the predatory strategies that disproportionately harm
children, young people, and people with alcohol dependence.
Unfortunately, this bill doesn't align with the solutions that most New Zealanders
support.
Instead, we're concerned this bill will increase harm by increasing alcohol
availability, diminishing people's right to have a say over
alcohol-related issues in their area, and perversely increasing
red tape for local councils and district licensing committees.
The consequences of these changes, if enacted, would be felt by the friends,
whanau, and parents losing their loved ones, including their children, to
physical and mental health conditions.
The women and children experiencing alcohol-related violence.
The people living with lifelong disabilities such as fetal alcohol spectrum
disorder or impairments from alcohol-related accidents such as drunk
driving.
The bill's intentions don't just contradict the government-commissioned evidence on
harm reduction that's been repeated since the 2010 Law Commission review.
They're a slap in the face to the people experiencing the consequences of a
regulatory framework that drives lifelong, intergenerational, and
inequitable harms that ultimately impact all of us.
When alcohol is everywhere, these harms can and do happen to
anyone,
but it doesn't have to be this way. Most New Zealanders support strengthening
our alcohol regulations, and we want to be very clear that this doesn't mean
prohibition. Instead, it's about protecting our loved ones from harm.
And from the first day of submissions, we know even the alcohol industry agrees
with changes that could do so. For example, 9:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m.
opening hours and stronger online delivery regulations, including a
24-hour safety pause between ordering and
delivery. We also support the bill's proposal to
strengthen online sales and delivery protections.
And while it's a modest start, we'd like to emphasize that this should extend to

solutions that have been available to us for a long time,
including having higher standards for the alcohol industry lobbying and alcohol
sales and advertising, especially against strategies targeting
children, young people, and highly deprived communities.
